content.text: Resource IDs

Every Azure resource has a globally unique, hierarchical identifier called a resource ID. It encodes the subscription, resource group, resource provider namespace, resource type, and resource name in a single slash-delimited path. ARM uses this ID as the canonical key for every API operation — GET, PUT, DELETE, and RBAC role assignments all operate on resource IDs.

attrs.alt: Azure resource ID anatomy: subscription, resource group, provider namespace, type, and name segments

content.text: ID Structure and Scope Levels

The structure varies depending on where in the Azure hierarchy the resource lives. Most resources are resource-group scoped. Some (like role assignments, policy assignments, and resource groups themselves) are subscription-scoped. Management groups and tenant-level resources omit the subscription segment entirely.

# Full resource ID anatomy — every segment explained
/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/{namespace}/{type}/{name}

# Example: Virtual Machine
/subscriptions/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
  /resourceGroups/myapp-rg
  /providers/Microsoft.Compute
  /virtualMachines/myvm

# Child resource (VM extension under a VM):
/subscriptions/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
  /resourceGroups/myapp-rg
  /providers/Microsoft.Compute
  /virtualMachines/myvm
  /extensions/AzureMonitorLinuxAgent

# Sub-resource (blob inside a storage container):
/subscriptions/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
  /resourceGroups/myapp-rg
  /providers/Microsoft.Storage
  /storageAccounts/mystorageacct
  /blobServices/default
  /containers/mycontainer

# Subscription-scoped resource (no resourceGroups segment):
/subscriptions/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
  /providers/Microsoft.Authorization
  /roleAssignments/yyyyyyyy-yyyy-yyyy-yyyy-yyyyyyyyyyyy
Scope
ID Pattern
Example Types
Tenant
/providers/{namespace}/{type}/{name}
Management Groups, AAD B2C
Subscription
/subscriptions/{subId}/providers/{namespace}/{type}/{name}
Role Assignments, Policy Assignments, Resource Groups
Resource Group
/subscriptions/{subId}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/{ns}/{type}/{name}
VMs, Storage, SQL, AKS, App Service
Child Resource
.../{type}/{name}/{childType}/{childName}
VM Extensions, Storage Containers, SQL Databases

Resource IDs are case-insensitive by convention. ARM normalizes casing internally, but the CLI and SDK return IDs in a consistent lowercase form. Always use case-insensitive comparison when matching resource IDs in code.
Retrieving Resource IDs
The Azure CLI's --query flag with id extracts the resource ID from any show or list command. Use -o tsv to get a plain string without quotes.

# Get the resource ID of a resource group
az group show --name myapp-rg --query id -o tsv

# Get the resource ID of a specific resource
az vm show --name myvm --resource-group myapp-rg --query id -o tsv

# List all resource IDs in a resource group
az resource list --resource-group myapp-rg --query '[].id' -o tsv

# Get IDs filtered by resource type
az resource list \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --query '[].id' -o tsv

# Cross-subscription: get resource ID by name across all subscriptions
az resource list \
  --name myvm \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --query '[].{id:id, sub:id | split("/")[2]}' -o table
Parsing Resource IDs
Bash

Split on '/' and index into the segments. Segment positions are fixed for resource-group-scoped resources: index 2 is subscription ID, index 4 is RG name, index 6 is namespace, index 7 is type, index 8 is name.
Python
A general-purpose parser handles all scope levels and child resources by walking the key/value pairs in the ID:

def parse_resource_id(resource_id: str) -> dict:
    """
    Parse an Azure resource ID into its components.
    Handles subscription-scope, RG-scope, and child resources.
    """
    parts = resource_id.strip('/').split('/')
    result = {}

    # Map known segment keys to their values
    i = 0
    while i < len(parts) - 1:
        key = parts[i].lower()
        value = parts[i + 1]
        if key == 'subscriptions':
            result['subscription_id'] = value
        elif key == 'resourcegroups':
            result['resource_group'] = value
        elif key == 'providers':
            result['namespace'] = value
        else:
            # Remaining pairs are type/name segments
            result.setdefault('types', []).append(key)
            result.setdefault('names', []).append(value)
        i += 2

    result['resource_type'] = '/'.join(result.get('types', []))
    result['resource_name'] = result.get('names', [''])[-1]
    return result

# Example
rid = '/subscriptions/xxxx/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm'
print(parse_resource_id(rid))
# {'subscription_id': 'xxxx', 'resource_group': 'myapp-rg',
#  'namespace': 'Microsoft.Compute', 'resource_type': 'virtualmachines',
#  'resource_name': 'myvm'}
Constructing Resource IDs in IaC
Bicep

Bicep provides built-in functions for constructing resource IDs without string concatenation. Use resourceId() for RG-scoped resources, subscriptionResourceId() for subscription-scoped resources, and tenantResourceId() for tenant-scoped resources.
ARM Templates
Terraform
Querying Resource IDs at Scale with Azure Resource Graph
The Azure CLI az resource list is scoped to one subscription and is slow at scale. Azure Resource Graph (ARG) queries across all subscriptions in a tenant in seconds using KQL syntax.

# Azure Resource Graph: query resource IDs at scale across subscriptions

# Find all VMs with a specific tag across all subscriptions
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| where tags.environment == 'production'
| project id, name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
" --query 'data[].id' -o tsv

# Find orphaned NICs (not attached to any VM)
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type == 'microsoft.network/networkinterfaces'
| where isnull(properties.virtualMachine)
| project id, name, resourceGroup
" -o table

# Batch lookup: get resource type from a list of IDs
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where id in~ ('<id1>', '<id2>', '<id3>')
| project id, type, name
"
Resource IDs as Scope for RBAC and Diagnostics

RBAC role assignments and diagnostic settings both use resource IDs as their scope parameter. This makes the resource ID the central reference point for access control and observability — scoping both as narrowly as possible (individual resource vs. RG vs. subscription) follows least-privilege principles.
RBAC Scoping with Resource IDs

# RBAC role assignments target a specific resource ID as the scope

# Grant Contributor on a specific resource group
az role assignment create \
  --assignee user@example.com \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg

# Grant Reader on a specific VM only
az role assignment create \
  --assignee user@example.com \
  --role Reader \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm

# Grant Storage Blob Data Reader on a specific container
az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id <managed-identity-object-id> \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystore/blobServices/default/containers/mycontainer

# List all role assignments for a resource
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm \
  --include-inherited -o table
Diagnostic Settings and Private Endpoints

# Diagnostic settings reference the resource ID they monitor
RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm'
LOG_ANALYTICS='/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/monitoring-rg/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myworkspace'

az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $RESOURCE_ID \
  --name vm-diag \
  --workspace $LOG_ANALYTICS \
  --metrics '[{"category":"AllMetrics","enabled":true}]'

# Private endpoint: resource ID is the target for the private link
az network private-endpoint create \
  --name myvm-pe \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --vnet-name myvnet \
  --subnet default \
  --private-connection-resource-id $RESOURCE_ID \
  --connection-name myvm-connection \
  --group-id management
Use of Resource ID
Where It Appears
Notes
RBAC scope
az role assignment --scope
Inherited by child resources; child IDs also work
Diagnostic settings
az monitor diagnostic-settings --resource
Each resource has its own diag settings
Private endpoint target
az network private-endpoint --private-connection-resource-id
Links the endpoint to the specific resource
Policy exemption scope
az policy exemption --scope
Exempts just that resource from a policy
Azure Monitor alerts
az monitor metrics alert --scope
Alert fires on metrics from that specific resource
When Resource IDs Change

Resource IDs are not permanent. Several operations produce a new resource ID for the same logical resource. Any system storing resource IDs (monitoring dashboards, Terraform state, application configs) must be updated when a move or recreation occurs.

# Resource IDs change in these scenarios:
# 1. Resource move (az resource move) — ID gets new RG segment
# 2. Subscription transfer — ID gets new subscription segment
# 3. Resource recreation (delete + create) — same name, new GUID if any
# 4. Terraform taint + apply — forces recreation, new ID

# Detect stale resource ID references after a move
OLD_RG='myapp-old-rg'
NEW_RG='myapp-new-rg'

# Check if any resources in the new RG still reference the old RG in their properties
az resource list --resource-group $NEW_RG -o json | \
  python3 -c "
import json, sys
resources = json.load(sys.stdin)
old_rg = '$OLD_RG'.lower()
for r in resources:
    props = json.dumps(r.get('properties', {})).lower()
    if old_rg in props:
        print(f\"Stale reference: {r['name']} ({r['type']})\")  
"
Resource move (az resource move): the /resourceGroups/{name} segment changes to reflect the new group
Cross-subscription transfer: the /subscriptions/{subId} segment changes
Delete and recreate with the same name: the ID looks identical but refers to a new resource — GUIDs in sub-resources (e.g. role assignment GUIDs) will differ
Terraform taint + apply: forces a destroy/recreate cycle; the new resource gets a new ID
ARM template redeployment in Complete mode: may delete and recreate resources that change type, generating new IDs
Fetching and Deleting by Resource ID with the Python SDK

The ResourceManagementClient provides get_by_id and begin_delete_by_id methods that accept any resource ID without needing separate type-specific clients. You must supply the correct API version for the resource type.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# Fetch any resource by its full ID
resource_id = (
    f'/subscriptions/{subscription_id}'
    '/resourceGroups/myapp-rg'
    '/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm'
)

resource = client.resources.get_by_id(
    resource_id,
    api_version='2023-03-01'
)
print(resource.name, resource.type, resource.location)

# Delete any resource by its full ID
poller = client.resources.begin_delete_by_id(
    resource_id,
    api_version='2023-03-01'
)
poller.result()
Resource IDs in Azure Policy

Azure Policy exemptions are scoped to specific resource IDs. Policy rule conditions can also match on the 'id' field using contains or like operators to target resources by naming pattern within their ID.

# Azure Policy: reference specific resource IDs in exemptions

# Exempt a specific resource from a policy assignment
az policy exemption create \
  --name myvm-exemption \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myvm \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/<subId>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/my-policy \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'Legacy VM exempt from OS patch policy'

# Policy condition: match resources by ID pattern
# (ARM Policy supports field 'id' conditions)
POLICY_RULE='{
  "if": {
    "field": "id",
    "contains": "/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-"
  },
  "then": { "effect": "audit" }
}'
Summary

Azure resource IDs are the canonical addressing scheme for every ARM resource: structured as /subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/{namespace}/{type}/{name}. Child resources append additional type/name segments. Use the CLI's --query id to retrieve IDs, Bicep's resourceId() to construct them in templates, and Azure Resource Graph for cross-subscription ID lookups at scale.
